name: heritage_seed_bank_patent_claim
premise: Seed-bank director Asha Menon discovers that a biotech company has filed patents on drought-resistant traits found in heritage millet varieties her nonprofit preserved from smallholder farmers over the past twenty years. The company claims its lab work transformed scattered traits into a marketable climate-resilient crop and says patent protection is necessary to fund large-scale distribution. Farmer cooperatives argue that once the patents take effect, they may have to pay licensing fees to plant seeds descended from material their own communities protected through repeated droughts. Asha can challenge the patent publicly now, risking the collapse of a partnership that would bring improved seed access to millions, or sign a quiet benefit-sharing agreement that secures discounted access for some regions while leaving the core ownership claim intact. Her board is split between fighting on principle and accepting a compromise that might help farmers sooner.

agents.name: Asha Menon
agents.identity: I am the director of a nonprofit seed bank built on trust with farming communities that believed preservation would keep their genetic heritage in common stewardship. I understand how urgently climate adaptation is needed, but I also know ownership rules shape who remains free to farm.
agents.goal: I want farmers to gain practical access to resilient seed without legitimizing a property regime that privatizes community-preserved traits. I need to decide whether compromise secures equality or surrenders it.

agents.name: Victor Hale
agents.identity: I am the biotech company's head of partnerships and I have spent years turning fragile traits into stable cultivars that regulators and distributors will actually support. Without patents, investors will walk and farmers may never see these improved seeds at meaningful scale.
agents.goal: I want Asha to sign the benefit-sharing deal because imperfect access now is better than principled stalemate that leaves climate-vulnerable farmers waiting. I believe private incentives can coexist with public benefit if structured carefully.

choices: I will publicly challenge the patent claim because farmers should not lose control over traits preserved through their own stewardship, even if it jeopardizes near-term distribution.
I will sign the benefit-sharing agreement so farmers gain discounted access to the improved seed now, even though it leaves the company's ownership claim largely intact.
forced_choice.call_to_action: Asha Menon must decide whether to fight the patent claim publicly or accept a compromise agreement. What does Asha Menon do?
